name: Yongma Land Abandoned Theme Park
description: Plenty of old carney magic still haunts this photogenic amusement park.

name: Coffee Hanyakbang
description: About as hidden as can be, this specialty coffee shop is wedged between multi-story buildings at the end of a dark, skinny alleyway in Seoul.

name: Imsil Cheese Theme Park
description: A 32-acre fun park dedicated to all things cheese.

name: Museum Kimchikan
description: Tracking the 1,500-year history of Korea's star cabbage.

name: Jeju Loveland
description: South Korea's only sex-themed park.

name: Third Tunnel of Aggression
description: Secret North Korean passage discovered below the world's most dangerous border.

name: Daewonsa Temple
description: To enter the lower hall of this temple, you must first walk through a dragon.

name: Banpo Bridge Moonlight Rainbow Fountain
description: The world's longest bridge fountain has hundreds of nozzles that create a beautiful, illuminated spectacle.

name: Gonjiam Psychiatric Hospital
description: The abandoned hospital whose elaborate ghost stories cover up the dirty truth - an uneventful sanitation issue and other mundane reasons for its demise.

name: Oedo Botania
description: One man and his wife have turned a barren Korean island into a fantastical botanical wonderland.

name: Japanese Ruins of Busan
description: The 400-year-old remains of the samurai invasion of Korea.

name: Seodaemun Prison
description: A chilling monument to Korean patriots who resisted the Japanese occupation.

name: 108 Heaven Stairway
description: A remnant of Korea's colonial past lies forgotten in a hip neighborhood in Seoul.

name: Owl Art and Craft Museum
description: One man's lifetime collection of tiny objects of wisdom gathered from around the world, on display for public enjoyment.

name: Noryangjin Fish Market
description: Buy your fish and have it cooked to order in this bustling, eight-floor seafood market.

name: Dreamy Camera Cafe
description: This quirky Korean eatery is built to resemble an equally quirky vintage camera.

name: Haesindang Park
description: Various forms of phallic art decorate this hill in commemoration of legendary local tragedy.

name: Jinkwansa Temple
description: An all-female order shares an ancient culinary art with visitors at their mountaintop monastery.

name: Hyangwonjeong Pavilion
description: A unique structure located on the grounds of one of five grand palaces that were built by the Joseon dynasty.

name: Naganeupseong Folk Village
description: A Korean village where times stands still.

name: Suguksa Temple
description: The name of the only golden Buddhist temple in Korea translates as "the temple that protects the country."

name: Hwaam Cave
description: Asia's largest limestone cave is home to a world of plastic goblins and gold mining history.

name: Samtan Art Mine
description: This former coal mine has been turned into a sprawling modern art complex.

name: Tripitaka Koreana
description: Over 80,000 carved wood blocks make up one of the world's oldest intact Buddhist canons.

name: Vantablack Hyundai Pavilion
description: The darkest building on Earth was built for the snow-white scenery of the 2018 Winter Olympics.

name: Seoul Book Repository
description: Collected from all around Seoul, thousands of secondhand books create this photogenic tunnel.

name: Heyri Art Valley
description: A thriving artist's wonderland located just 6 kilometers from Korea's DMZ.

name: Tapsa Temple
description: 120 mortarless pagodas built by one Korean hermit.

name: Grasshopper's Dream Cafe
description: Along a rail-biking route, giant insects house a two-story restaurant.

name: Jeju Mermaids
description: South Korea's women of the sea have completely flipped their society's structure by becoming revered fisherwomen.

name: War Memorial of Korea
description: Home to more than 13,000 pieces of war memorabilia and military equipment.

name: Dora Observatory
description: The best place for scenic views of the DMZ and North Korea.

name: Sewoon Sangga (Sewoon Plaza)
description: Its humdrum facade only adds to its electrifying appeal: a rooftop with panoramic views of a shapeshifting metropolis.

name: Wongaksa Pagoda
description: This pagoda was once the central feature of a Buddhist temple that no longer exists.

name: Gyeongui Line Book Street
description: Booksellers and public artworks fill this vibrant promenade along the disused railroad tracks.

name: Sun Cruise Resort and Yacht
description: The world's first cruise ship-shaped hotel is perched atop a cliff.

name: Jindo-Modo Landbridge
description: Landbridge created by the South Korean version of Moses.

name: Seonjeongneung
description: The burial ground of two kings and a queen is now a calm city park in the middle of bustling Seoul.

name: Haedong Yonggungsa
description: One of only a handful of seaside temples in Korea, this sacred space is home to a legendary healing Buddha statue and a traffic safety prayer pagoda.

name: Cheomseongdae
description: Numerical symbolism abound at East Asia's oldest observatory.

name: Rock It Suda
description: A Korean rock bassist's wild dream has become a reality at this completely insane boutique hotel.

name: Jaman Mural Village
description: Throughout this South Korean village, homes and businesses are covered in fantastic handpainted murals.

name: Kosik the talking elephant
description: Seoul amusement park boasts a Korean-speaking pachyderm.

name: Jongmyo Shrine
description: The supreme shrine of the state.

name: Wolji Pond
description: This human-made pond is part of a Korean palace complex first built in the 7th century.

name: Sky Pizza
description: Visit the real-life site of the fictional pizzeria in Bong Joon-Ho's film Parasite.

name: Andong Hahoe Folk Village
description: A traditional village rooted in the Joseon Dynasty.

name: Mr. Toilet House
description: The toilet-shaped house of South Korea's sanitation pioneer is now a museum to the john.

name: Magoksa Temple
description: Ancient temple is home to centuries of art and spiritual artifacts.

name: Jusanji Pond
description: Trees emerge from the surface of this small man-made reservoir.

name: Dorisa Temple
description: Known as the birthplace of Korean Buddhism, this temple features an ancient, five-story stone pagoda.

name: 38th Parallel Beach
description: A thriving surf community exists in relative remoteness along the former boundary between North and South Korea.

name: Pohang Space Walk
description: This 317-ton walkable art installation is an homage to Korea's "City of Steel."

name: Borugak Jagyeongnu
description: Only a few pieces remain of a grand 16th-century water clock that once announced the changing hours with the sound of bells, gongs, and drums.

name: Dokdo/Takeshima (Liancourt Rocks)
description: Japan and South Korea both lay claim to islands inhabited by one woman.

name: Namdaemun
description: A symbol of hope, survival, and national character, especially after it rose phoenix like from the flames.

name: Jjajang Myeon Museum
description: This tiny museum immortalizes the birthplace of an iconic Chinese-Korean noodle dish.

name: Teddy Bear Museum
description: The history of the plush toy and South Korea collected under one roof.

name: Hampyeong Expo Park
description: It’s a bug’s life at this Korean eco-event park, built to host an annual butterfly festival.

name: National Hangeul Museum
description: Celebrating the history of Hangul, the Korean writing system invented by King Sejong to improve his realm’s literacy.

name: Pink Muhly Grass
description: From mid-September to early November, dreamy fields of pink muhly grass spring up all over South Korea.

name: Hwangudan
description: The site where the Emperor of Korea performed the long-forbidden "rite of heaven" to demonstrate his absolute sovereignty.

name: Chamsori Gramophone and Edison Science Museum
description: Explore the history of recorded sound at the world's third largest collection of Edison memorabilia.

name: Unhyeongung
description: The site of the last royal wedding in Korea.

name: Dongpirang Mural Village
description: This hillside village by the sea has been revitalized by street art.

name: Gatbawi Rock
description: According to legend, those who pray at this statue will also be granted a wish.

name: Naminara Republic
description: This small island declared independence from South Korea in the hopes of attracting tourists.

name: Imjingak Resort
description: A large visitor's complex and place of remembrance on the outskirts of the DMZ.

name: Wang Dong's Rock Quarries
description: Rocks that take on the likenesses of several recognizable living entities crowd the Wang dong Rock Quarries.

name: Gatbawi
description: Natural rock formations resembling monks in hats inspire folk legends.

name: Dilkusha House (Albert W. Taylor House)
description: The former home of a foreign correspondent who reported on Korea’s independence movement has been restored and is open to the public.

name: Jangdo Island
description: Low tide reveals an ocean bridge to an island full of art.

name: Palsangjeon
description: The only wooden pagoda remaining in South Korea is part of a Buddhist temple that was established in the year 553.

name: Gyeonggyojang House
description: The headquarters of Korea’s provisional government later became the site of a prominent statesman's assassination.

name: Son Kee Chung’s Greek Helmet
description: This ancient Corinthian helmet is South Korea’s only national treasure of Western origin.

name: Cheonmachong
description: This tomb's name derives from a famous painting of a white horse found inside.

name: Sangpyeongtongbo Gallery
description: A small section in the Bank of Korea Museum dedicated to the many varieties of the Joseon dynasty’s iconic coinage.

name: Stone Pagoda of Hwaeomsa
description: An unusual pagoda design in in the northwestern part of Hwaeomsa Temple.

name: Dosan Seowon
description: This school was constructed in memory of Korean Confucian scholar Yi Hwang.

name: Gyeongju Seokbinggo
description: An early Korean refrigerator.

name: World’s Largest Department Store
description: The Shinsegae Department Store is the largest of its kind in the whole world.

name: Lee Jang-woo's House
description: This traditional home is a rare example of 19th-century Korean architecture.

name: Sinsedong Seven-Story Brick Pagoda
description: The largest and oldest brick pagoda in Korea.

name: Seokguram Grotto
description: Visiting this grotto is symbolic of a spiritual journey into Nirvana.

name: May 18th National Cemetery
description: This cemetery is dedicated to the people who died fighting for democracy in South Korea.

name: Gentle Monster
description: A pair of oily black spiders lurk inside South Korea's largest airport.

name: Guryongpo Japanese House Street
description: Originally built by Japanese fishermen, this historic neighborhood has withstood the test of time.

name: Bunhwangsa
description: This grand temple complex from the Old Silla era of Korea is home to a stone pagoda that was build in the 7th century.

name: Camp Greaves
description: An arts and culture complex that was once a derelict American military installation in the civilian control zone of the DMZ.

name: Poseokjeong Pavilion
description: All that remains of an ancient royal palace is a water channel that was used for drinking games.

name: Donggureung
description: The largest cluster of the Joseon dynasty’s royal tombs, home to the burial sites of 17 kings and queens.

name: ‘Minor Triumph’
description: Children’s timeless joy is remembered in the fast-developing Myeongdong district of Seoul.

name: Seoksu from the Tomb of King Muryeong
description: A guardian spirit sculpture that once guarded the tomb of a 6th-century Korean king.

name: Illsan Lake Park
description: Home to the largest artificial lake in Asia.

name: ‘Jangdokdae’
description: A family of distorted sculptures stands outside Seoul's former imperial palace.

name: Hwangsang-dong Buddha Carving
description: Though it is hundreds of years old, the intricate details on this 23-foot rock carving of Buddha have been preserved.

name: Odusan Unification Observatory
description: The place nearest Seoul where it’s easy to get a good look at North Korea.

name: Hwajinpo Summer Houses
description: The triumvirate of postwar Korea—Kim Il-sung, Syngman Rhee, and Lee Ki-poong—once vacationed on this seaside peninsula.

name: Hwangnyonsa Temple Site
description: Only the stone foundations remain of one of the greatest Buddhist temples in early East Asian history.

name: Military Cemetery for North Korean Soldiers
description: A seldom-visited resting place for North Korean soldiers and spies.

name: Greem Cafe
description: Step into a cartoon at this Seoul café that aims to evoke a two-dimensional scene.

name: Trick Eye Museum
description: South Korea's love of the silly photograph is on full display at this goofy collection of trompe l'oeil paintings.

name: Jeju Glass Castle Theme Park
description: A theme park made of glass, from the gleaming books on the shelves to the crystal fish in the mirror lake.

name: Poopoo Land
description: A bright and wacky exhibit answers every question you may have had about human excrement, flatulence, and more.

name: Metro Farm
description: Some of the freshest vegetables in Seoul are grown in a subway station.

name: Love Castle
description: This odd museum/art gallery embraces the physiological, zoological, and ethnographic side of sex.

name: Bridge of No Return
description: The site of many tense prisoner exchanges across the Military Demarcation Line between North and South Korea.

name: Dorasan Station
description: South Korea's largely symbolic, barely used gateway to the North.

name: Korean Axe Murder Incident Memorial
description: A monument to the two U.S. officers killed by North Korean troops while trying to trim a poplar tree.
